reasoning: The wp_ajax_nopriv_prdctfltr_respond_550 handler is confirmed reachable by unauthenticated users (line 38), and set_search_post_ids_from_ajax() at lines 55-70 contains no current_user_can or is_user_logged_in check. However, the finding's own justification concedes the vulnerability only materialises 'if the search engine does not filter by post status.' The search engine does filter: Engines/WordPressNative/Search.php line 218 sets 'post_status' => 'publish' inside getSearchResults(), meaning only published products can ever appear in results. Private, draft, and password-protected products are excluded by the WP_Query args before any results are returned. Furthermore, the handler is intentionally nopriv — it exists to support anonymous storefront visitors filtering products on a public WooCommerce shop page; unauthenticated access is the designed use-case, not a defect. Because the backend search is fully restricted to publicly visible products, there is no information leakage and no access control violation. The finding is a false positive.
